pattern: A single post-order walk that prunes deleted nodes can also recognize a surviving node as a new root exactly when its parent was deleted, collecting the forest in one pass.
hints: When you delete a node, each of its surviving children becomes the root of its own tree, so the parent's deletion status is what creates new roots.
Recurse returning each node or None after removing deleted ones; a node is a new forest root when it survives but its parent did not.

Pass down whether the parent was deleted; on the way back, detach deleted children by returning None, and record a surviving node as a root when its incoming parent was deleted (the original root counts as having a deleted parent).

edge_inputs.description: delete two internal nodes splitting into three trees
delete the root itself promoting its children
empty delete list leaves the tree intact
delete every node leaves an empty forest

stress.description: random tree with distinct values and a random delete subset
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 200)))
    vals = rng.sample(range(1, 1001), n)
    arr = [vals[0]]
    # build a level-order tree where each slot may be a remaining value or null
    pool = vals[1:]
    pi = 0
    while pi < len(pool):
        if rng.random() < 0.8:
            arr.append(pool[pi]); pi += 1
        else:
            arr.append(None)
    while arr and arr[-1] is None:
        arr.pop()
    present = [v for v in arr if v is not None]
    k = rng.randint(0, len(present))
    to_delete = rng.sample(present, k) if k else []
    return [arr, to_delete]
stress.note: values are distinct; delete set is a subset of present values
